conditions at Goldfields are great
ly exaggerated" said George Buhrson
of Tekoa, who has just returned from
the great gold camp. The stories
sent out of epidemics were un-
founded.


I'm the first place. Goldfelds is not
a poor mans camp. There is no
placer work and it takes money and
machinery to develop the mines.
When the fever was on men rushed
in there with barely enough money
to reach the goal. They were paupers
at once, lived filthy lives, were poor
ly fed and after the hot days would
sleep on the cold ground In tents at
night and take no care of themselves.
Liquor ana the cold ground at nights
gave them pneumonia and they began
dropping off like sheep long before
anyone knew what was the matter.
